posts.name: David Kubiak
posts.cooked:
I bought a bottle of ‘03 Lafite from a very reliable merchant. It was described as ‘ex-Château’. What I got surprised me. Everything about the bottle looked new: the bottle itself, label, and especially the fill, which is about a quarter inch from the cork. It has a curious hard plastic cover over the capsule with a French tax stamp on it. It is hard for me to believe that this bottle was resting at the Château for 20 years. Can someone explain what it is I have?
Thanks.
David Kubiak

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked: Would have been stored as a “shiner” and labelled and capsuled just before shipping - that’s quite standard.

posts.name: Rich K0rz€nk0
posts.cooked:
Every now and then I see something I somehow missed in this wine thing we do. Was not familiar with the term “shiner” until here (Thx) and the great Google enlightening me thereafter.

Wine Spectator

Shiners Are a Secret Part of the Wine Business | Wine Spectator

Shiners are a big part of the wine business. But most people have never heard of them, even though they drink shiners all the time. That’s because shiners get a complete makeover and these otherwise often undistinguished wines are disguised by their...

link_counts.title: Shiners Are a Secret Part of the Wine Business | Wine Spectator

posts.name: Jon Lawrence
posts.cooked:
I have some ex-chateaux '82 Ducru that were rebouché in 2011 and released in 2022. Nice shiny labels and no concern of TCA. I have an '00 ex-chateau Lynch (2021 release) on deck for tonight. When buying older stock it is nice to find stuff with this kind of provenance.

IMG_19711920×2560 469 KB

IMG_19721920×2560 413 KB

posts.name: David Kubiak
posts.cooked:
image3024×4032 3.34 MB

image3024×4032 2.57 MB

image3024×4032 2.47 MB

Forgive the kitchen picture. If the wine was just recently bottled where has it been kept for 20 years?
Thanks again to the experts.

posts.name: Kris_P@tten
posts.cooked: It wasn’t recently bottled, may have been recorked and recently labelled, as William said above they store shiners in cellar and label as needed so the labels don’t rot in cellar or get dirty.

posts.name: Nicholas C00per
posts.cooked:
If ex-chateau release I have always understood that the bottles are stored at the Chateau and are released from their reserves. Typically a shiner as William Kelley commented above.

Enjoy the wine!

posts.name: Keith Levenberg
posts.cooked: I doubt they’d have recorked a 2003 and I’m pretty sure Lafite labels recorkings when they do it. The good fill is just because it’s been well-stored, that’s the way it should be. The French tax stamps on the other hand imply at least one European buyer between Lafite and the US importer.

posts.name: Doug Schulman
posts.cooked:
Keith_Levenberg:

The French tax stamps on the other hand imply at least one European buyer between Lafite and the US importer.

Maybe, but not necessarily. Sometimes those are applied to an entire lot when a producer is not sure how much will be sold in France versus outside.

posts.name: Ian Dorin
posts.cooked: Even without a picture, everything you describe is 100% consistent with every ex-Chateau Lafite bottle I’ve ever seen. I was at Zachys when they did the 150th anniversary sale, so I got to see bottles all the way back to 1868!
